coredata.dc:title: Enhancing the stability of DSSC by Co-activation of microwave synthesized TiO2 with biomass derived carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ninan, Gisa Grace
Varghese, Meera
Balachandran, Manoj
coredata.dc:description: Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) that utilize natural dyes have garnered interest due to their low cost, eco-friendly manufacturing process, and competitive photovoltaic performance. However, their efficiency and stability issues have hindered their widespread implementation. To enhance their performance, this paper proposes a novel approach of modifying the photoanode with carbon dots (CDs) to align the band gap for easier carrier collection. The material properties were thoroughly characterized by examining their structural, morphological, optical, and electrical properties. In this study, titanium dioxide (TiO2) was synthesized using the microwave-assisted solvothermal method, while nitrogen-doped CDs derived from Citrus medica fruit juice were prepared using a simple hydrothermal treatment. Three sets of Natural Dye Sensitized Solar Cells (NDSSC) devices were created using co-activated photoanode (CD/TiO2) and unmodified photoanode (TiO2) with Platisol T/sp coated ITO serving as the counter electrode. Hibiscus (Hibiscus rosa-sinensis) and Onion (Allium cepa) peel extracts were utilized as sensitizers and Iodolyte HI-30 as the electrolyte. The most efficient device attained an efficiency of 3.5 % with Voc = 0.81 V and Jsc = 6.57 mA/cm2. This marks the highest efficiency reported using Hibiscus as a sensitizer with the current configuration, accompanied by prolonged device stability. This study showcases the potential of Citrus medica-derived nitrogen-doped CDs in achieving durable device stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanium dioxide
Microwave synthesis
Carbon dots
DSSC
Natural dyes
Citrus medica

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of photoanode using microwave assisted solvothermal method
Hibiscus and onion peel dye extraction

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the Centre for Research, and central instrumentation facility (CIF), CHRIST (Deemed to be University) for research fund file no.: SMSS-2221 and for the instrumentation facilities. The authors also like to express their gratitude to SAIF-STIC, CUSAT (Kerala) for the characterization facilities. The authors would also like to acknowledge DST-FIST for providing the research fund under SR/FST/PS-I/2022/208(C).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: schematic illustration of the synthesis of Citrus medica-derived CDs.
The molecular structure of an anthocyanin compound bound to TiO2 surface.
a) TEM image of CD/TiO2 and histogram of particle size distribution for CDs (inset), b) SEM image of TiO2 nanoparticles c), EDS spectra of CD/TiO2.
a) XRD of TiO2 nanoparticles and CDs/TiO2 composite b) FTIR spectra of CDs.
a) UV–visible absorption spectra of CD, TiO2 and CD/TiO2 b) Tauc plot of CD, TiO2 and CD/TiO2.
a) The J-V graph obtained for CD/TiO2 devices with hibiscus dye up to 60 days. b) The stability comparison of both TiO2 and CD/TiO2 devices with hibiscus dye.
a) The J-V graph obtained for CD/TiO2 devices with onion peel dye up to 60 days. b) The stability comparison of both TiO2 and CD/TiO2 devices with onion peel dye.
Photovoltaic performance of DSSC using various natural dyes.
Output parameters obtained for CD/TiO2 devices with hibiscus dye up to 60 days.
Output parameters obtained for CD/TiO2 devices with onion peel dye up to 60 days.
Photovoltaic performance and stability of present DSSC compared with previous literature.

head.ce:title: Enhancing the stability of DSSC by Co-activation of microwave synthesized TiO2 with biomass derived carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Electronics, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru, Karnataka, 560029, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Electronics, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bengaluru, Karnataka, 560029, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) that utilize natural dyes have garnered interest due to their low cost, eco-friendly manufacturing process, and competitive photovoltaic performance. However, their efficiency and stability issues have hindered their widespread implementation. To enhance their performance, this paper proposes a novel approach of modifying the photoanode with carbon dots (CDs) to align the band gap for easier carrier collection. The material properties were thoroughly characterized by examining their structural, morphological, optical, and electrical properties. In this study, titanium dioxide (TiO2) was synthesized using the microwave-assisted solvothermal method, while nitrogen-doped CDs derived from Citrus medica fruit juice were prepared using a simple hydrothermal treatment. Three sets of Natural Dye Sensitized Solar Cells (NDSSC) devices were created using co-activated photoanode (CD/TiO2) and unmodified photoanode (TiO2) with Platisol T/sp coated ITO serving as the counter electrode. Hibiscus (Hibiscus rosa-sinensis) and Onion (Allium cepa) peel extracts were utilized as sensitizers and Iodolyte HI-30 as the electrolyte. The most efficient device attained an efficiency of 3.5 % with Voc = 0.81 V and Jsc = 6.57 mA/cm2. This marks the highest efficiency reported using Hibiscus as a sensitizer with the current configuration, accompanied by prolonged device stability. This study showcases the potential of Citrus medica-derived nitrogen-doped CDs in achieving durable device stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Apart from the stable efficiency achieved with silicon solar cells, Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs) offer numerous advantages that continue to drive research in this field. DSSCs are considered as an emerging photovoltaic technology due to their promising properties, including cost-effectiveness, transparency, flexibility, superior performance in low-light conditions, wide bandgap tunability, and simple fabrication processes. These characteristics make DSSCs a potential high-efficiency alternative to traditional solar cells. A standard DSSC consists of four main components: photoanode, sensitizer, electrolyte and counter electrode.

Research in the fabrication of DSSCs, particularly Natural Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (NDSSCs), has gained attraction due to several key factors. These include the ease of device fabrication, cost savings from replacing synthetic dyes with natural sensitizers, and the potential for modifications by altering device components to use eco-friendly and non-toxic materials. These aspects not only enhance the sustainability of the technology but also open up new avenues for improving the overall efficiency and performance of DSSCs [1]. The use of natural dyes as photosensitizers in DSSCs is due to their efficient light absorption properties and facile preparation. Unlike expensive and environmentally unfriendly inorganic dyes, natural dyes are affordable, non-toxic and contain diverse active groups, making them well-suited for DSSCs [2,3]. Anthocyanin, a prevalent component in natural dyes exhibits effective photosensitization capabilities. However, despite these advantages, the potential use of natural dyes as sensitizers in DSSCs is hindered by their relatively low efficiency values, as summarized in Table 1 .

In the present study, costly synthetic photosensitizers are replaced with natural Hibiscus dye and Onion peel dye which are biodegradable. The anthocyanin content present in these dyes can absorb light in the visible range [12,13]. As the next component, various studies have focused on the electrolyte and counter electrode to improve the stability of DSSCs [14,15]. In the present study Iodolyte HI-30 and Platisol T/sp are used as electrolyte and counter electrode respectively. Coming to the photoanode component, a novel approach has been investigated by modifying the photoanode with carbon dots (CDs) to enhance stability and overall performance. The structural, optical, and electrical properties of TiO2 make it a preferred nominee for the device fabrication of DSSC [16]. Some of the techniques followed for the preparation of TiO2 are sol-gel [17], hydrothermal [18], ultrasonic [19], and Microwave-assisted solvothermal method [20]. It has been reported that the particle size and phases present in TiO2 play a crucial role in dye loading and light scattering. If the particle size is very large, electron diffusion length and light scattering increase, but dye loading decreases [21]. The efficiency of the device largely depends on parameters such as surface area and the density of hydroxyl groups, which can be tuned through different synthesis methods [22]. The nano aggregates of TiO2 prepared using a microwave synthesizer can improve charge transfer and dye loading properties due to their greater surface area [23]. In the present study, we obtained mixed phases (Anatase and Rutile) of TiO2 via a microwave-assisted solvothermal method with suitable properties for better device output. Another main advantage of microwave-assisted synthesis is the rapid and uniform heating that happens in microwave device. It helps in rapid crystallization, reducing the total temperature and reaction time [24]. A shorter reaction time can reduce the contamination of products formed to a larger extent.

Further modifications to DSSCs can be achieved using various CDs. CDs in DSSCs can serve multiple roles, including as a sensitizer, co-sensitizer, or as a co-photoactive layer [25]. In the latter role, the incorporation of CDs into the photoanode system can induce synergistic effects, leading to increased light absorption and minimized electron-hole recombination [26,27]. Additionally, CDs provide numerous anchoring sites for the dye and facilitate charge transfer in mesoporous TiO2, thereby enhancing the device performance. CDs can also improve the photoconversion efficiency and stability of DSSCs [28,29]. One of the significant challenges in DSSC performance is the degradation of the photosensitizer and associated stability issues. CDs can absorb UV light from incident solar radiation and down-convert it to the visible region, which can be utilized by the photosensitizer without degradation effects [30–32]. Furthermore, the synthesis of CDs is simple, less expensive and eco-friendlier compared to other inorganic quantum dots (QDs). CDs are highly biocompatible with low toxicity and elevated surface area [33,34]. Recently nitrogen-doped carbon dots have emerged as a promising co-active material for DSSC photoanodes due to their superior optical properties and enhanced charge transfer capabilities [35,36]. In this study, a simple, low-cost and eco-friendly hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize nitrogen-doped CDs from the fruit extract of Citrus medica. The obtained CDs were used as a co-activation material with the photoanode (CD/TiO2) and their performance was compared with an unmodified photoanode (TiO2). The prepared devices were evaluated using the J-V characteristic plot under AM1.5G 100 mW/cm2 illumination.

Another significant challenge with DSSCs is their device stability, which has been a focal point of extensive research. Despite advancements in improving voltage and current density, DSSCs often suffer from rapid degradation, leading to a substantial decline in efficiency within a few days of post-fabrication. The present study also focuses on stability studies that were conducted for up to 60 days of post-fabrication, to assess the longevity and performance retention of the devices.

The TiO2 powders were synthesized using a microwave synthesizer (Anton-Parr, Monowave 400). Structural characterization of the photoanode prepared with TiO2 was performed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (Rigaku-600 with Cu-Kα radiation, λ = 1.54 Å). The Scherrer formula (D = 0.9λ/β cos θ) was employed to calculate the crystallite size and density of the synthesized TiO2 particles. The morphology of TiO2 was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Thermo Fisher Scientific Apreo S LoVac) and Jeol/JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM). Optical properties were investigated using a UV–Visible spectrometer (Ocean Optics, JAZA 3228) and a fluorescence and absorbance spectrometer (Duetta, Horiba Scientific). The J-V characteristics were measured using a solar simulator and a Keithley source meter (Photo Emission Tech Inc. Solar Simulator SS50AAA-EM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of photoanode using microwave assisted solvothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: The TiO2 hydrosol was synthesized as follows. Initially, 12 ml of deionized water was heated to 80° C and a few drops (0.4 ml) of nitric acid were added to it. This solution was then subjected to magnetic stirring for half an hour. Simultaneously, a mixture of titanium isopropoxide and acetic acid was prepared separately in the ratio of 2.4: 2 and added to the above mixture. A white precipitate was obtained and subsequently heated for an additional 10 min to evaporate the acetic acid. The mixture was then transferred to a microwave vessel sealed with a silicon septum. After reacting at 80 °C for 10 min, a translucent suspension was obtained, which was then dried overnight at 80 °C to yield TiO2 crystals. These crystals were ground in a mortar for 30 min to produce a fine powder of TiO2 nanoparticles.

The synthesis of Citrus medica-derived CDs was achieved by a facile hydrothermal method. 1 ml of NH3 was added to 40 ml of Citrus medica fruit extract and magnetically stirred for 1 h. The mixture was then transferred to a 50 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at a temperature of 180° C for 12 h. The recovered brown colored carbonized solution was then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30 min to remove unreacted particles. The supernatant was then filtered through 0.22 μm nylon membrane and then preserved in the refrigerator for future use. Fig. 1 shows the schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs from the aqueous extract of Citrus medica fruit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hibiscus and onion peel dye extraction
ce:section.ce:para: The quality of natural dye mainly depends on its possibility to absorb the visible light and is done by certain chemical groups present in it. The functional groups like carboxyl (-COOH) and hydroxyl (-OH) help in the attachment of dye to TiO2 and also help in electron transfer [37]. Fig. 2 schematically illustrates the bonding between the anthocyanin pigment and the TiO2 surface.

The ethanol was used for extraction because anthocyanin is a phenolic polar compound that dissolves better in a polar solvent. About 10g of dried petals of Hibiscus were well crushed in mortar and is transferred into a beaker and soaked in ethanol for 24 h. It was then squeezed, filtered, and heated for 1 h at 40° C to obtain the dye solution and was stored in refrigerator after cooling down. Similarly, 10g of cleaned and dried onion peels were taken and is soaked in water for 24 h at 90°C. The extract is filtered and heated for 1 h at 80°C to obtain concentrated dye and was stored in refrigerator for further studies.

Two grams of synthesized titania powder, 1 ml of acetic acid, 0.5 ml of carbon dots (CDs) solution, and 0.8 ml of Triton X-100 were ground in an agate mortar and pestle for 45 min to obtain a homogenous colloidal paste. For the pure photoanode, the same procedure was followed without the addition of CDs. The indium-doped tin oxide (ITO) substrate (resistivity 10 Ω/sq) was ultrasonically cleaned using ethanol and deionized water for 30 min and then dried before the photoanode coating. A uniform paste was applied over a 0.25 cm2 area of the ITO substrate using the doctor blading method. The as-prepared films were gradually annealed to a temperature of 350 °C and then allowed to cool down to 80 °C before being immersed in the prepared Hibiscus dye. After 48 h, the photoanodes were removed and rinsed with ethanol to wash off any unbound dye. Counter electrodes were prepared by coating Platisol T/sp on another ITO substrate using the doctor blade technique, followed by sintering at 350 °C for 1 h. After cooling, the complete fabrication of DSSCs was achieved by sandwiching the dye-absorbed photoanode and the counter electrode with binder clips, after injecting the Iodolyte HI-30 electrolyte (purchased from Solaronix S.A.).

The current-voltage (I–V) characteristics of DSSCs were acquired using a solar simulator in conjunction with a source meter. A solar simulator, designed to replicate sunlight, provided the light source for illuminating the DSSC. Typically, the solar simulator operates at a standard light intensity of 1000 W/m2, simulating the AM 1.5G spectrum. The DSSC was connected to a source meter, such as the Keithley source meter, capable of applying a voltage across the cell and measuring the resulting current. The source meter applied a voltage sweep across the DSSC, ranging from short-circuit condition to maximum voltage open-circuit condition. At each voltage step, the source meter recorded the corresponding current, resulting in a series of (I–V) data points. These data points were then plotted to generate the I–V curve. Stability studies were conducted from day 1 of fabrication to day 60 in 10-day intervals. Three sets of each device were prepared to minimize error bars, and the best device parameters were presented.

The surface morphology of CD/TiO2 was confirmed from the TEM analysis (Fig. 3 a) and that of TiO2 from SEM analysis (Fig. 3b). The TEM image demonstrates the effective incorporation of CDs within the TiO2 matrix, maintaining a spherical morphology due to the adhesive nature of CDs. Although some particles exhibit size variations, the overall morphology remains consistent, with visible pores between the spherical structures. The average particle size of the CDs, determined from the histogram of particle size distribution, is approximately 4–9 nm is shown in the inset of Fig. 3a. The reduced porosity of CD/TiO2, evident from TEM images, limits electrolyte diffusion through the porous channels, thereby decreasing the likelihood of device short-circuiting. The spherical morphology of TiO2 nanoparticles is further confirmed through SEM analysis. SEM images reveal that the particle size of TiO2 ranges from 12 to 16 nm, consistent with XRD data. The particles predominantly appear as aggregates with a spherical morphology, which is attributed to the single nucleation process facilitated by microwave synthesis [38]. The use of water as a solvent contributes to the formation of compact agglomerates with good packing density, enhancing the path length of incident light and thereby improving carrier generation [39]. The Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis of CD/TiO2 was performed to identify the elemental composition of the composite material. As Fig. 3c shows the CD/TiO2 composite is composed of 17.64 Atomic % of carbon, 5.87 Atomic % of nitrogen, 60.27 Atomic % of oxygen and 16.23 Atomic % of Titanium.

The structural properties of TiO2 and CD/TiO2 powder obtained were characterized using XRD, as depicted in Fig. 4 a. The structural analysis is critical to understand different parameters required for the preparation of photo anode in order to acquire good photo conversion efficiency of the DSSCs. From the XRD Diffractograms, it is clear that the peaks corresponding to the Anatase phase occurs at (101), (004), (200), (211), (204) at angles (2θ) 26.08°, 34.4°, 48.7°, 63.45° respectively. The Rutile phase is obtained at (110), (101), (111), (211), and (220) at angles (2θ) 28.8°,42.6°, 47.1°, 54.6°, 55.9°. The tetragonal structure of the prepared TiO2 is confirmed from these diffraction patterns. The obtained XRD peaks confirm the mixed phase formation of Anatase and Rutile TiO2 (JCPDS no. 21–1272) (JCPDS no. 21–1276). With the addition of CDs, we could see a shift towards the right of all the XRD peaks, which may be due to the presence of carbon in the photoanode. The shift observed in XRD peaks resulting from the incorporation of carbon dots is attributed to the interaction between the carbon dots and TiO2 during composite formation. This interaction induces a mismatch in the lattice parameters of TiO2 due to lattice strain, leading to peak shifts in the diffraction pattern. Additionally, the chemical interaction between the carbon dots and TiO2 results in the formation of chemical bonds, altering the local electronic environment and contributing further to the observed shift in XRD peaks. Most of the recent literature supports the formation of mixed-phase advantageous for solar device fabrication. The Rutile phase and Anatase phase mixed TiO2 can be easily prepared using microwave synthesis. With the traditional synthesizing method, the Rutile phase is less formed due to low oxygen vacancies. The presence of high gaseous oxygen concentration during the growth time hinders the conversion of the Rutile phase from the Anatase phase. The mass fraction of Anatase and Rutile phases can be estimated using the general equation given below.
WA= KAAA/ (KAAA + KRAR)
WR= KRAR/(KAAA + KRAR)
where WA and WR are the fraction of the Anatase phase and Rutile phase [40]. The total intensity of Anatase and Rutile peaks is given by AA and AR. The AA and AR for CD/TiO2 were calculated and found to be 1823.00 and 515.32, respectively, whereas for unmodified TiO2 it is 1243.70 and 408.40. KA and KR are the coefficients with a constant value of 0.8. Using the above calculation, the percentage of Rutile phase present in CD/TiO2 is found to be 26 % and that of unmodified TiO2 to be 29 %. Thus, we can conclude that there is no much variation in the phase percentage with the addition of CDs. The average crystallite size is calculated using Debby Scherrer formula D= (kλ/β cos θ) where D is the crystallite size, K is Scherrer's constant (K = 0.94), λ is the wavelength of X-ray (1.54178 Å), β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the most substantial diffraction peak and the average crystallite size for TiO2 is found to be ±22 nm.

The FTIR analysis of CDs revealed the presence of various peaks, signifying the presence of distinct functional groups (Fig. 4b). The distinctive broad absorption band at 3293 cm-1 indicates the stretching vibration of the OH functional group, which originates from either water or alcohol [41,42]. Moreover, the band acquired at 1648.41 cm−1 can be Amide I band corresponds to the stretching vibration of the CO bond [43]. The peak observed at 1465 cm−1 indicates the C–H bending of the methylene group [44]. Additionally, the symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate are highlighted at 1391 cm−1, while the band at 1125.97 cm−1 confirms the C–N stretching vibrations [45,46]. Lastly, the peak obtained at 943.23 cm−1 indicates the O–O stretching modes [47]. The functional groups like hydroxyl and carboxyl groups present on the carbon dots introduce many adsorption sites and will act as dye anchors. Additionally, they improve the charge transfer properties by acting as electron acceptors [48–52].

The optical properties of the samples were characterized using UV–visible and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Fig. 5 a displays the absorbance curves for TiO2, CDs, and CD/TiO2. TiO2 shows strong absorption in the ultraviolet region due to its wide bandgap, while CDs exhibit broad absorption ranging from the UV to visible regions. In the UV–visible absorption spectrum of CDs, the absorption band at 276 nm corresponds to the π–π* transition of the C═C bond, and the peak at 312 nm is due to the n−π* transition of the C═O bond, indicating the presence of oxygenic groups such as carboxyl groups in the CDs. The addition of CDs to TiO2 extends the absorption range of the composite material, leading to improved short-circuit current density in DSSCs by enhancing photon harvesting and carrier generation. Fig. 5b shows the Tauc plots for CD, TiO2, and CD/TiO2. The relation for calculating the bandgap can be written as:
α = A (h υ − E g)n ∕h υ
Here, α is linear absorption coefficient, hν is photon energy, A is the constant for proportionality, and exponent n is a constant depending on indirect or direct band gap material. The band gap of TiO2, CDs and CD/TiO2 are calculated using the Tauc plots and found to be around 3.7 eV, 3.5 eV and 3.4 eV respectively. Nitrogen doping favors the formation of numerous active sites in the carbon dot structure, with reduced band gap and enhanced light absorption and photo conversion efficiency [53–57].

The photoluminescence spectra of CDs (excitation wavelengths 300 nm–500 nm) and CD/TiO2 (excitation wavelengths 280–340 nm) were recorded and maximum emissions were observed for 380 nm and 340 nm excitations peaked at ∼ 465 nm and 407 nm respectively. The TiO2 nanoparticles did not show any fluorescence in the absence of CDs however, the addition of CDs contributed to the PL properties. The PL spectra of the CD/TiO2 (Figure S1 a) showed a blue shift compared to that of CDs (Figure S1 b) with a PL quenching, which can be attributed to the improved photocatalytic activity resulted by the interfacial charge transfer between CDs and TiO2.

The device output characteristics are obtained from J-V analysis. Fig. 6 a depicts the J-V characteristics of the modified device sensitized with hibiscus dye (CD/TiO2(H)). On analyzing the Figure, we could see that the devices with co-activated photo anode are stable, with almost constant current density. This shows that even after 60 days, the dye is able to absorb the light efficiently and transfer the carriers without any loss. A slight Voc decrease is observed, which might be due to the electrolyte evaporation that happens as the days proceed. But in the presence of CDs, this evaporation of electrolytes is reduced comparatively. Fig. 6b presents a bar graph displaying the efficiencies of DSSC devices employing TiO2 and CDs/TiO2 photoanodes, sensitized with hibiscus dye, over a period of 60 days, with measurements taken at 10-day intervals.

As mentioned, stability measurements of the device from the J-V parameters obtained every 10 days up to 60 days are tabulated in Table 2 . All the output parameters are checked for both devices prepared using unmodified photo anode and co-activated photo anode, respectively. The device fabricated with unmodified TiO2 and sensitized with Hibiscus dye (TiO2(H)) exhibited an open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.66 V, short-circuit current density (Jsc) of 6 mA/cm2, and a fill factor (FF) of 51.6 % with an efficiency of 2.04 %. After eight days, the devices were shorted and showed no output results. Whereas the devices with CDs-TiO2 photo anode showed an open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.81 V, short-circuit current density (Jsc) of 6.57 mA/cm 2, and a fill factor (FF) of 65.9 % with an efficiency of 3.5 % with device stability for more than 60 days.

Similarly, Fig. 7 a depicts the J-V characteristics of the devices sensitized with onions dye and Fig. 7b illustrates a bar graph depicting the efficiencies of both modified (CD/TiO2(O)) and unmodified (TiO2(O)) DSSC devices, sensitized with onion peel dye over a span of 60 days, with measurements recorded at 10-day intervals. The unmodified TiO2 device displayed an open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.59 V, a short-circuit current density (Jsc) of 6.45 mA/cm2, and a fill factor (FF) of 55.18 %, resulting in an efficiency of 2.1 %. However, after eight days, these devices shorted and showed no output. In contrast, the devices with CDs-TiO2 photoanodes exhibited an open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.82 V, a short-circuit current density (Jsc) of 6.38 mA/cm2, and a fill factor (FF) of 65.9 %, achieving an efficiency of 3.5 % and maintaining stability for over 60 days. Table 3 clearly indicates that the current density of the co-activated devices are almost stable and there is only a slight decrease in the Voc, which might be due to the electrolyte evaporation that happens as the days proceed. But in the presence of CDs, this evaporation of electrolytes is reduced comparatively.

Fig. S2 displays the J-V graph used to calculate the efficiency statistics for three devices, with the corresponding graphical representation of efficiencies provided in Fig. S3. The labels TiO2(H):D1, TiO2(H):D2, and TiO2(H):D3 represent unmodified devices sensitized with hibiscus dye. represent unmodified devices sensitized with hibiscus dye, while CD/TiO2(H):D1, CD/TiO2(H):D2, and CD/TiO2(H):D3 refer to modified devices sensitized with hibiscus dye. Similarly, TiO2(O):D1, TiO2(O):D2, and TiO2(O):D3 stand for unmodified devices sensitized with onion peel dye, and CD/TiO2(O):D1, CD/TiO2(O):D2, and CD/TiO2(O):D3 represent modified devices sensitized with onion peel dye. There is an increment in the open circuit voltage, fill factor, and efficiency with the co-activation of the photo anode. The nitrogen presence in the CDs can improve the photon harvesting and photo conversion properties of the device to enhance the overall efficiency of the device. The excitation-dependent PL characteristics of CDs, which almost cover the visible region, provide a wide light response and high photon-to-electron injection ratio. Moreover, the modified TiO2 band gap and formation of new energy levels achieved through the incorporation of CDs can boost electron transfer from LUMO level of the dye to the conduction band of TiO2. These intermediate trap states suppress the electron-hole recombination between photoanode and the electrolyte. The abundant carboxylic groups on the surface of CDs adhere to the TiO2 and enhance the photocurrent density to improve the electron transport of the material [58]. The improvement in stability and stable Jsc with aging is due to enhanced transportation of carriers. The decrement in Voc is less compared to normal devices, which highlights that the CDs have the ability to inhibit the recombination of carriers at interface of TiO2/electrolyte. Both these factors, in turn help to keep the efficiency stable.

The P–V curves of the fabricated DSSCs are presented in Fig. S4a and Fig. S4b, corresponding to hibiscus dye and onion peel dye. According to the findings, the modified devices yielded the best output power, which is directly related to the output parameters obtained from the J-V curve.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) parameters were calculated for both unmodified and modified devices sensitized with Hibiscus dye and Onion peel dye to understand the charge transfer and recombination rates of photoexcited electrons in the fabricated devices. Nyquist plots obtained for the DSSC devices sensitized with Hibiscus dye and Onion peel dye are depicted in Figs. S4c and S4d, respectively. The resulting EIS parameters are presented in Table S1. The tabulation reveals that the ohmic serial resistance (Rs) remains nearly constant for both modified and unmodified devices, indicating uniformity and optimal performance of the prepared photoanodes with minimal contact resistance across all samples. The second semicircle observed in the Nyquist plot represents the charge transfer resistance at the photoanode/electrolyte interface (Rct2), which reflects the kinetics of the oxidation reaction at the photoanode. Compared to the unmodified devices, the lower Rct2 value obtained for the modified devices suggests that the incorporation of carbon dots reduced the charge transfer resistance, facilitating easier electron transport on the modified photoanode surface. This reduction in charge transfer resistance contributes to improved photocurrent and enhanced efficiency in the modified devices compared to the unmodified ones, consistent with the J-V results obtained. Fig. S5 shows the Efficiency of the fabricated DSSC devices as a function of time and Table 4 presents a comparison of the photovoltaic performance and stability of the fabricated devices with data from recent reports. The results obtained in the present work is very important which can help to solve one of the main problems regarding NDSSC fabrication. Being a preliminary study, it opens a novel way for further research.

Two types of NDSSCs were fabricated in this study - one with an unmodified TiO2 photoanode, and the other with a photoanode co-activated with CDs. The highest efficiencies obtained for the best devices, CD/TiO2 and TiO2, respectively, were ∼3.5 % and ∼2 %. These efficiencies are the highest obtained and reported so far with Hibiscus dye and Onion peel dye as the sensitizer in this device configuration. The improvement in efficiency can be attributed to the increased dye loading capacity, which resulted in better Jsc. This was confirmed by SEM analysis, which showed agglomerated large spherical structures. The dense aggregate formation increased the electron diffusion length, which helped improve the output parameters of the device. Stability studies conducted for both devices revealed that the TiO2 devices were stable for only up to 8 days, whereas CD/TiO2 devices were stable for up to 60 days. The stability improvement of CD/TiO2 was attributed to the improved transportation of carriers and the ability of CDs to inhibit carrier recombination at the TiO2/electrolyte interface.

Gisa Grace Ninan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Meera Varghese: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Manoj Balachandran: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Centre for Research, and central instrumentation facility (CIF), CHRIST (Deemed to be University) for research fund file no.: SMSS-2221 and for the instrumentation facilities. The authors also like to express their gratitude to SAIF-STIC, CUSAT (Kerala) for the characterization facilities. The authors would also like to acknowledge DST-FIST for providing the research fund under SR/FST/PS-I/2022/208(C).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Properties of nitrogen-doped amorphous hydrogenated carbon films
J. Appl. Phys.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Amir, R. Kalish, Properties of nitrogen-doped amorphous hydrogenated carbon films, J. Appl. Phys. 70 (1991) 4958-4962. https://doi.org/10.1063/1.349043.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Doped TiO2 and TiO2 nanotubes: synthesis and applications
ChemPhysChem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.C. Nah, I. Paramasivam, P. Schmuki, Doped TiO2 and TiO2 nanotubes: Synthesis and applications, ChemPhysChem. 11 (2010) 2698-2713. https://doi.org/10.1002/cphc.201000276.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots as Co-active materials for highly efficient dye sensitized solar cells
Carbon N. Y.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.P. Shejale, A. Jaiswal, A. Kumar, S. Saxena, S. Shukla, Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots as Co-active materials for highly efficient dye sensitized solar cells, Carbon N. Y. 183 (2021) 169-175.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Long-run performance of dye-sensitized solar cell using natural dye extracted from Costus woodsonii leaves
Opt. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Various impacts of blocking layer on the cell stability in natural dye based dye-synthesized solar cell
Optik
